description: Cloud-focused threat actor profiles. Includes groups with demonstrated cloud exploitation capability — from social engineering specialists to nation-state actors with advanced cloud persistence techniques.
verificationNote: Threat actor profiles based on publicly available threat intelligence reports from Mandiant, CrowdStrike, Microsoft Threat Intelligence, Unit42, and CISA advisories. MITRE ATT&CK technique IDs reference the Enterprise matrix (cloud-relevant techniques).

threatActors.name: SCATTERED SPIDER
threatActors.attribution: Loosely organised group of English-speaking threat actors, primarily US and UK based, aged 16-25. Some members linked to The Com (online criminal community). Multiple arrests in 2023-2024.
threatActors.motivation: Financial — ransomware deployment, data extortion, cryptocurrency theft, SIM swapping
typicalTTPs: SMS phishing (smishing) impersonating IT helpdesk with fake SSO login pages
Social engineering calls to IT helpdesks to reset MFA or add attacker device
SIM swapping to intercept SMS-based MFA codes
MFA fatigue attacks (push notification spam)
Okta admin console compromise to create persistent access
Azure AD/Entra ID manipulation — adding federated identity providers for persistent access
Cross-tenant lateral movement via compromised MSP/reseller accounts
Deployment of BlackCat/ALPHV ransomware in cloud environments
Living-off-the-land using native cloud tools (Azure Run Commands, AWS SSM)
keyIncidents: MGM Resorts (Sep 2023) — social engineered IT helpdesk to reset Okta MFA, deployed BlackCat ransomware, $100M+ impact
Caesars Entertainment (Sep 2023) — social engineering attack, $15M ransom paid
Twilio (Aug 2022) — smishing campaign against employees, accessed customer data including Authy/Signal
Cloudflare (Aug 2022) — smishing attack against employees, blocked by FIDO2 hardware keys
0ktapus campaign (2022) — phished over 130 organisations targeting Okta SSO credentials

primary: Phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2/WebAuthn) — eliminates SMS, push, and TOTP phishing entirely. Cloudflare's use of hardware keys blocked Scattered Spider when other companies fell.
Helpdesk identity verification procedures — out-of-band verification for all MFA resets and account changes
Conditional access policies requiring compliant devices and trusted locations
secondary: Number matching for push MFA (reduces fatigue attacks, but does not eliminate them)
SIM swap protection with carrier PIN/passphrase
Privileged Identity Management (PIM) with approval workflows for admin role activation
Cloud audit log monitoring for IdP configuration changes (federation, MFA policy changes)

threatActors.name: APT29 / Cozy Bear
threatActors.attribution: Russia — Foreign Intelligence Service (SVR). Attributed by US, UK, and EU governments.
threatActors.motivation: Strategic intelligence collection — foreign policy, government communications, technology sector intelligence
typicalTTPs: Supply chain compromise (SolarWinds SUNBURST — most significant cloud supply chain attack to date)
Golden SAML — forging SAML tokens using stolen AD FS signing certificates for persistent Azure AD access
OAuth application abuse — creating malicious OAuth apps with mail.read permissions for persistent mailbox access
Password spraying against cloud authentication endpoints at scale
Residential proxy networks to evade geographic-based conditional access policies
Compromising cloud service providers and MSPs to access downstream customer environments
Token theft and session replay for cloud service access
Long-term persistent access — dwelling for months to years in victim cloud environments
keyIncidents: SolarWinds/SUNBURST (2020) — supply chain compromise affecting 18,000 organisations, Golden SAML used for Azure AD persistence at US government agencies
Microsoft corporate breach (Jan 2024) — password spraying against legacy test account without MFA, accessed senior leadership email via OAuth application permissions
TeamViewer corporate breach (Jun 2024) — compromised employee account to access corporate IT environment
Multiple European government and diplomatic entity compromises (2021-2024) — persistent cloud email access via OAuth apps

primary: Protect SAML/OIDC signing keys with HSM — never allow signing key material in crash dumps, backups, or accessible file systems
Audit all OAuth/enterprise application consent grants — alert on new applications with mail.read, mail.readwrite, or directory.read permissions
Eliminate legacy authentication protocols (basic auth, SMTP AUTH) — APT29 exploits these to bypass MFA
secondary: Conditional access requiring MFA for all accounts including service accounts
CloudTrail / Entra audit log monitoring for token anomalies and impossible travel
Regular review of federated identity provider configurations
Supply chain security — verify software integrity, monitor build pipelines

threatActors.name: LAPSUS$
threatActors.attribution: Loosely organised group of young threat actors, initially based in UK and Brazil. Multiple members arrested in 2022, including a 16-year-old from Oxford, UK.
threatActors.motivation: Notoriety, financial gain, and destruction — primarily motivated by attention and demonstrating capability rather than pure financial return
typicalTTPs: Purchasing credentials and session tokens from insiders and dark web markets
SIM swapping for MFA bypass
Social engineering IT helpdesks for credential and MFA resets
Searching internal code repositories and wikis for hardcoded credentials after initial access
Enrolling personal devices in corporate MDM/MFA to establish persistent access
Joining internal Slack/Teams channels to monitor incident response in real time
Publicly announcing breaches on Telegram for attention and pressure
keyIncidents: Microsoft (Mar 2022) — compromised employee account, accessed Azure DevOps repos containing partial source code for Bing, Cortana, and Bing Maps
Okta (Jan 2022) — compromised a Sitel (support subcontractor) engineer's workstation, accessed Okta customer support tools affecting ~2.5% of customers
NVIDIA (Feb 2022) — stole 1TB of data including proprietary GPU designs and employee credentials
Samsung (Mar 2022) — exfiltrated 190GB of source code including Galaxy device security software
Uber (Sep 2022) — MFA fatigue attack on contractor, accessed AWS, GWorkspace, Slack, HackerOne (attributed to LAPSUS$-affiliated individual)
Rockstar Games (Sep 2022) — leaked GTA VI development footage, arrested

primary: Phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2) — eliminates SIM swap and social engineering MFA bypass
Zero trust architecture — device compliance checks, no VPN-only perimeter
Secrets scanning in code repositories (GitHub Advanced Security, GitGuardian) to detect hardcoded credentials before attackers find them
secondary: Insider threat monitoring — alert on bulk data downloads, unusual repository access
Contractor and third-party access segmentation — contractors should not have broad internal access
Incident response channel security — assume adversary is monitoring Slack/Teams during incidents
SIM swap protection with carrier-level controls

threatActors.name: TeamTNT
threatActors.attribution: Financially motivated cybercriminal group, suspected German-speaking members based on code comments and infrastructure.
threatActors.motivation: Financial — cryptocurrency mining using stolen cloud compute resources, credential theft for further access
typicalTTPs: Scanning for exposed Docker daemons (TCP 2375/2376) and Kubernetes API servers
Exploiting misconfigured Docker API to deploy cryptomining containers
Stealing AWS credentials from instance metadata (IMDSv1) and ~/.aws/credentials files
Deploying XMRig (Monero) cryptocurrency miners on compromised containers and VMs
Worm-like propagation scanning internal networks for additional Docker/K8s targets
Using Weave Scope (legitimate Kubernetes monitoring tool) as backdoor dashboard
DNS-over-HTTPS for C2 communication to evade network monitoring
Credential scraping from environment variables, Docker configs, and Kubernetes secrets
keyIncidents: Hildegard campaign (2021) — targeted misconfigured Kubernetes clusters, deployed cryptominers, stole cloud credentials, used IRC-based C2
AWS credential theft campaign (2020) — first threat group publicly documented stealing AWS credentials from compromised Docker instances
Chimaera campaign (2021) — expanded targeting to include Windows containers, Redis, and Kubernetes orchestrators
Multiple cryptojacking campaigns (2020-2023) — continuous operations targeting exposed container infrastructure globally

primary: Never expose Docker daemon to the network — Docker API must require authentication and TLS
Kubernetes API server must not be publicly accessible — use private cluster endpoints
IMDSv2 enforcement on all EC2 instances to prevent metadata credential theft
secondary: Container runtime security (Falco) detecting cryptominer deployment and unexpected network scanning
Network egress filtering blocking mining pool connections
Regular scanning for exposed Docker/K8s endpoints (Shodan, internal scanning)
Pod security standards preventing privileged containers and host namespace access

threatActors.name: ShinyHunters
threatActors.attribution: Cybercriminal group, with at least one confirmed member (Sebastien Raoult, French national, extradited to US and sentenced in 2024). Multiple other members believed active.
threatActors.motivation: Financial — data theft and sale on dark web markets, extortion of victim organisations
typicalTTPs: Targeting misconfigured cloud storage (S3 buckets, Azure Blob containers) for data theft
Compromising developer accounts on GitHub to access private repositories containing credentials
Exploiting exposed .git directories on web servers to recover source code and embedded secrets
Phishing campaigns targeting developers with access to cloud infrastructure
Accessing cloud databases through credentials found in source code repositories
Bulk data exfiltration and sale on BreachForums, RaidForums (defunct), and Telegram
keyIncidents: AT&T / Snowflake (2024) — part of the broader Snowflake customer breach campaign, accessed 109 million AT&T phone records
Ticketmaster / Snowflake (2024) — accessed 560 million customer records from Ticketmaster's Snowflake instance
Microsoft GitHub (2022) — claimed to have stolen 37GB of Microsoft source code from internal repositories
Tokopedia (2020) — 91 million user records stolen and sold
Wattpad (2020) — 270 million user records compromised
Mashable (2020) — database breach and leak
Multiple cryptocurrency exchanges — targeting hot wallet credentials in cloud environments

primary: Block public access on all cloud storage at organisation level (S3 BPA, Azure storage account public access disabled)
Secrets scanning in all repositories — pre-commit hooks and CI/CD pipeline scanning (GitHub Advanced Security, GitGuardian, TruffleHog)
MFA on all developer accounts — especially GitHub, GitLab, and cloud console accounts
secondary: Regular external attack surface assessment (exposed .git directories, misconfigured storage, leaked credentials)
CASB for shadow SaaS discovery and data exfiltration monitoring
Developer security training emphasising credential hygiene and cloud security basics
Automated rotation of any credential detected in source code

threatActors.name: Muddled Libra
threatActors.attribution: English-speaking threat actors, significant overlap with Scattered Spider. Mandiant tracks as UNC3944. The distinction is primarily analytical — Muddled Libra emphasises the identity-centric cloud attack pattern while Scattered Spider emphasises the social engineering tradecraft.
threatActors.motivation: Financial — data theft, extortion, ransomware deployment. Specific focus on identity provider compromise as enabler for broad cloud access.
typicalTTPs: Identity provider (IdP) targeting as the primary objective — compromising Okta, Entra ID, or other IdP grants access to all federated applications
Helpdesk social engineering for MFA reset — impersonating employees with spoofed caller ID and personal details from OSINT
Registering new MFA devices on compromised accounts for persistent access
Creating new admin accounts in IdP for backdoor access surviving password resets
Abusing SSO trust relationships to pivot from one SaaS application to all federated applications
Targeting IT administrators and security team members specifically (highest-value targets for IdP access)
Using cloud-native tools for reconnaissance (Azure AD Graph API, AWS IAM enumeration)
Data exfiltration from SaaS applications (SharePoint, Confluence, Slack) accessed via compromised IdP
keyIncidents: 0ktapus campaign (2022) — phishing campaign targeting Okta credentials at over 130 organisations, compromising approximately 10,000 user credentials
Multiple BPO/MSP compromises (2022-2024) — targeting business process outsourcing firms and managed service providers to access downstream customer environments
Cryptocurrency platform targeting (2022-2024) — systematic targeting of crypto exchanges and DeFi platforms via employee IdP compromise

primary: Phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2/WebAuthn) for all IdP administrator accounts — non-negotiable
Hardened helpdesk verification procedures — video call verification, manager approval, and out-of-band confirmation for all MFA resets
IdP configuration monitoring — real-time alerting on new admin accounts, MFA policy changes, federation configuration changes, and new OAuth application grants
secondary: Privileged Identity Management (PIM) with approval workflows for IdP admin role activation
Break-glass account procedures for IdP admin access (separate from daily operations, hardware-key MFA, monitored)
SSO session duration limits with forced re-authentication
Segment IdP admin access to dedicated, hardened workstations with no email or web browsing
